core_story.limitation: However, such models cannot produce high-resolution results with precise details.
core_story.insight: We observe that while the generated results may lack pre- cision in complex regions such as hands and faces, they can be effectively refined using localized 3D reconstruction methods [15, 39], which provide rich structural information.
core_story.proposed_method: In this work, we propose AudCast, a generalized audio-driven human video generation framework adopting a cascade Diffusion- Transformers (DiTs) paradigm, which synthesizes holistic human videos based on a reference image and a given au- †Corresponding Authors. dio.

core_story.summary: Particularly, alongside the usages of large language models [3, 48], virtual humans are crucial in boosting human-computer interaction experiences by pro- viding interactive visual appearances. However, such models cannot produce high-resolution results with precise details. We observe that while the generated results may lack pre- cision in complex regions such as hands and faces, they can be effectively refined using localized 3D reconstruction methods [15, 39], which provide rich structural information. In this work, we propose AudCast, a generalized audio-driven human video generation framework adopting a cascade Diffusion- Transformers (DiTs) paradigm, which synthesizes holistic human videos based on a reference image and a given au- †Corresponding Authors. dio. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework generates high-fidelity audio-driven holis- tic human videos with temporal coherence and fine facial and hand details.

introduction_structure.main_message: Introduction Nowadays, audio-driven virtual humans have played impor- tant roles in various scenarios, serving as virtual hosts, lec- turers, and salespeople.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Introduction Nowadays, audio-driven virtual humans have played impor- tant roles in various scenarios, serving as virtual hosts, lec- turers, and salespeople. Particularly, alongside the usages of large language models [3, 48], virtual humans are crucial in boosting human-computer interaction experiences by pro- viding interactive visual appearances. While great efforts have been paid to the field of audio-driven human anima- tion [18, 19, 28, 42, 58, 72, 73], most previous research fo- cus on controlling only the lip or torso areas [52, 53, 66]. Though lip-sync methods aim to blend lip movements into videos seamlessly, their body movements cannot be modi- fied, leading to inconsistent body rhythm and speech.

introduction_structure.main_message: More- over, showing only talking heads cannot satisfy the needs of most scenes.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: More- over, showing only talking heads cannot satisfy the needs of most scenes. It is essential to produce audio-coherent avatars with accurate lip-sync and rhythmic gestures. In order to generate real-world human videos with co- speech gestures, a few studies [21, 34] map audios to im- plicit motion representations [45] in warpping-based gen- erative models. However, such models cannot produce high-resolution results with precise details.

introduction_structure.main_message: Meanwhile, re- searchers have been exploring speech-driven gesture gener- ation [11, 35, 61, 64, 71, 75] in the representation of explicit 3D key points and body meshes.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Contrasts prior work and emphasizes unresolved weaknesses.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Meanwhile, re- searchers have been exploring speech-driven gesture gener- ation [11, 35, 61, 64, 71, 75] in the representation of explicit 3D key points and body meshes. However, the data in these works relies on 3D key point detection and 3D reconstruc- tion [38, 43], which could easily be inaccurate due to the ill-pose nature of single-view 3D reconstruction. The lim- ited availability of high-quality captured data restricts the broader applicability of these methods in general settings. As a result, despite the recent progress in 2D- and 3D-based conditional human video generation [10, 26, 27, 41] with diffusion models [7, 24, 46], only few studies have made the exploration of leveraging explicit audio-driven 2D and 3D intermediate representations to recover real-world humans.

introduction_structure.main_message: Particularly, Vlogger [13] proposes a two-stage framework with diffusion models and generates videos in a coarse-to- fine manner, but the movements are not natural enough.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Uses concrete examples to ground the narrative.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Particularly, Vlogger [13] proposes a two-stage framework with diffusion models and generates videos in a coarse-to- fine manner, but the movements are not natural enough. Inspired by recent success in text-based video genera- tion [62], that multi-modal condition enables high-quality generations with large-scale training, an ideal solution is to directly learn the mapping between audio and human videos, which is particularly challenging but promising. To facilitate this goal, two particular questions need to be an- swered: 1) How to build a powerful multi-modal generative backbone that suits audio-driven human video generation? 2) It is well-known that generating human animations, par- ticularly intricate elements such as finger gestures, remians a challenging task even in image synthesis.

introduction_structure.main_message: How do we pre- serve the fine details of holistic human appearance?
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Transitions from problem framing to the high-level solution.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: How do we pre- serve the fine details of holistic human appearance? In this work, we propose AudCast, the framework for Audio-Driven human video generation by Cascaded Dif- fusion Transformers with local refinement, which holis- tically produces expressive and realistic audio-driven hu- man dynamics from photos. Our key is to take advantage of both holistic audio-driven body movement generation and regional detail refinements with Diffusion Transformers (DiTs). Specifically, we build our model upon the recent success in text-to-video (t2v) generation, where DiT [40] has been proven effective in generating high-quality im- ages [16] and videos [62, 70] that are coherent with multi- modality conditions.

introduction_structure.main_message: Thus, we first propose the Holistic Human DiT (H 2-DiT) upon an existing t2v backbone and make concise yet principal modifications by involving the conditions of audio, appearance, and other functional in- puts, facilitating effective audio-driven animation that con- sistently preserves the target’s appearance and produces audio-coherent movements.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Introduces an observation that motivates the proposed direction.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Thus, we first propose the Holistic Human DiT (H 2-DiT) upon an existing t2v backbone and make concise yet principal modifications by involving the conditions of audio, appearance, and other functional in- puts, facilitating effective audio-driven animation that con- sistently preserves the target’s appearance and produces audio-coherent movements. However, even large-scale video generation models can- not handle delicate human details such as fingers. Simi- larly, our H 2-DiT might produce blurry results on details, particularly when the predicted gestures are complicated. We observe that while the generated results may lack pre- cision in complex regions such as hands and faces, they can be effectively refined using localized 3D reconstruction methods [15, 39], which provide rich structural information.

introduction_structure.main_message: Rather than explicitly utilizing intermediate 3D representa- tions as direct guidance for whole-body movement gener- ation [27, 76], which can constrain the natural dynamics of body motion, we instead leverage these structural priors solely for localized refinement.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Builds context and advances the paper's argument.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Rather than explicitly utilizing intermediate 3D representa- tions as direct guidance for whole-body movement gener- ation [27, 76], which can constrain the natural dynamics of body motion, we instead leverage these structural priors solely for localized refinement. The rendered 3D parts offer explicit guidance by supplying sufficient structural informa- tion, which benefits more fine-grained regional synthesis on the corresponding local areas. We thereby modify the DiT structure to a Regional-Refinement DiT (R2-DiT) that takes the 3D guidance to produce more realistic results with in- tricate details. Experiments show that the results from our cascaded DiTs clearly outperform previous studies.

introduction_structure.main_message: Our contributions can be summarized as follows: 1) We propose the AudCast framework to tackle the task of audio- driven human video generation, which produces expressive co-speech human videos with intricate details.
introduction_structure.writing_strategy: Enumerates takeaways to make the paper's value explicit.
introduction_structure.evidence_text: Our contributions can be summarized as follows: 1) We propose the AudCast framework to tackle the task of audio- driven human video generation, which produces expressive co-speech human videos with intricate details. 2) With straightforward yet effective design choices, we develop the Holistic Human DiT, which integrates multi-modal condi- tions and key foundational components to enable general- ized, long-duration human video generation with rhythmic motion and expressive expressions. 3) Leveraging local 3D reconstructions, we propose the Regional Refinement DiT, a model that enhances the outputs of the holistic video gener- ation through a cascaded process informed by 3D structural priors. This refinement yields noticeable improvements in fine details of the face and hands.

method_structure.purpose: Introduces part of the proposed approach.
method_structure.author_introduction_strategy: Presents the method incrementally from overview to components.

experiment_structure.purpose: Presents experimental evidence supporting the paper.
experiment_structure.author_introduction_strategy: Uses staged evidence from setup to comparison and analysis.

Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework generates high-fidelity audio-driven holis- tic human videos with temporal coherence and fine facial and hand details.

In this work, we propose AudCast, the framework for Audio-Driven human video generation by Cascaded Dif- fusion Transformers with local refinement, which holis- tically produces expressive and realistic audio-driven hu- man dynamics from photos.

Our contributions can be summarized as follows: 1) We propose the AudCast framework to tackle the task of audio- driven human video generation, which produces expressive co-speech human videos with intricate details.

3) Leveraging local 3D reconstructions, we propose the Regional Refinement DiT, a model that enhances the outputs of the holistic video gener- ation through a cascaded process informed by 3D structural priors.
Given a target video clip V = {Its , · · · , Ite}, our method oper- ates on the compressed visual latent features, represented by Z = E(V).
Next, we comprehensively present both quantitative and qualitative results to showcase the perfor- mance of our method.

We evaluate our method under a zero-shot protocol, meaning that the test inputs, includ- ing driving audio and reference frames, are entirely unseen during training, and there is no overlap in the subjects be- tween training and testing data.
It is important to note that our method remains in a zero-shot setting throughout this comparison, with no tuning specifically for this subject.
experiment_expression: Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework generates high-fidelity audio-driven holis- tic human videos with temporal coherence and fine facial and hand details.
Experiments show that the results from our cascaded DiTs clearly outperform previous studies.

Extensive comparisons with existing methods demonstrate the effectiveness of our cascaded DiT archi- tecture, offering valuable insights and setting a foundation for future research in conditional human video generation under more intricate scenarios.
contribution_expression: Our contributions can be summarized as follows: 1) We propose the AudCast framework to tackle the task of audio- driven human video generation, which produces expressive co-speech human videos with intricate details.

3) Leveraging local 3D reconstructions, we propose the Regional Refinement DiT, a model that enhances the outputs of the holistic video gener- ation through a cascaded process informed by 3D structural priors.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework generates high-fidelity audio-driven holis- tic human videos with temporal coherence and fine facial and hand details.
However, even large-scale video generation models can- not handle delicate human details such as fingers.

raw_sections.abstract:
Abstract
Despite the recent progress of audio-driven video genera-
tion, existing methods mostly focus on driving facial move-
ments, leading to non-coherent head and body dynamics.
Moving forward, it is desirable yet challenging to generate
holistic human videos with both accurate lip-sync and del-
icate co-speech gestures w.r.t. given audio. In this work,
we propose AudCast, a generalized audio-driven human
video generation framework adopting a cascade Diffusion-
Transformers (DiTs) paradigm, which synthesizes holistic
human videos based on a reference image and a given au-
†Corresponding Authors.
dio. 1) Firstly, an audio-conditioned Holistic Human DiT
architecture is proposed to directly drive the movements of
any human body with vivid gesture dynamics.2) Then to en-
hance hand and face details that are well-knownly difficult
to handle, a Regional Refinement DiT leverages regional
3D fitting as the bridge to reform the signals, producing
the final results. Extensive experiments demonstrate that
our framework generates high-fidelity audio-driven holis-
tic human videos with temporal coherence and fine facial
and hand details. Resources can be found at https:
//guanjz20.github.io/projects/AudCast.
This CVPR paper is the Open Access version, provided by the Computer Vision Foundation.
Except for this watermark, it is identical to the accepted version;
the final published version of the proceedings is available on IEEE Xplore.

raw_sections.introduction:
1. Introduction
Nowadays, audio-driven virtual humans have played impor-
tant roles in various scenarios, serving as virtual hosts, lec-
turers, and salespeople. Particularly, alongside the usages of
large language models [3, 48], virtual humans are crucial in
boosting human-computer interaction experiences by pro-
viding interactive visual appearances. While great efforts
have been paid to the field of audio-driven human anima-
tion [18, 19, 28, 42, 58, 72, 73], most previous research fo-
cus on controlling only the lip or torso areas [52, 53, 66].
Though lip-sync methods aim to blend lip movements into
videos seamlessly, their body movements cannot be modi-
fied, leading to inconsistent body rhythm and speech. More-
over, showing only talking heads cannot satisfy the needs
of most scenes. It is essential to produce audio-coherent
avatars with accurate lip-sync and rhythmic gestures.
In order to generate real-world human videos with co-
speech gestures, a few studies [21, 34] map audios to im-
plicit motion representations [45] in warpping-based gen-
erative models. However, such models cannot produce
high-resolution results with precise details. Meanwhile, re-
searchers have been exploring speech-driven gesture gener-
ation [11, 35, 61, 64, 71, 75] in the representation of explicit
3D key points and body meshes. However, the data in these
works relies on 3D key point detection and 3D reconstruc-
tion [38, 43], which could easily be inaccurate due to the
ill-pose nature of single-view 3D reconstruction. The lim-
ited availability of high-quality captured data restricts the
broader applicability of these methods in general settings.
As a result, despite the recent progress in 2D- and 3D-based
conditional human video generation [10, 26, 27, 41] with
diffusion models [7, 24, 46], only few studies have made the
exploration of leveraging explicit audio-driven 2D and 3D
intermediate representations to recover real-world humans.
Particularly, Vlogger [13] proposes a two-stage framework
with diffusion models and generates videos in a coarse-to-
fine manner, but the movements are not natural enough.
Inspired by recent success in text-based video genera-
tion [62], that multi-modal condition enables high-quality
generations with large-scale training, an ideal solution is
to directly learn the mapping between audio and human
videos, which is particularly challenging but promising. To
facilitate this goal, two particular questions need to be an-
swered: 1) How to build a powerful multi-modal generative
backbone that suits audio-driven human video generation?
2) It is well-known that generating human animations, par-
ticularly intricate elements such as finger gestures, remians
a challenging task even in image synthesis. How do we pre-
serve the fine details of holistic human appearance?
In this work, we propose AudCast, the framework for
Audio-Driven human video generation by Cascaded Dif-
fusion Transformers with local refinement, which holis-
tically produces expressive and realistic audio-driven hu-
man dynamics from photos. Our key is to take advantage
of both holistic audio-driven body movement generation
and regional detail refinements with Diffusion Transformers
(DiTs). Specifically, we build our model upon the recent
success in text-to-video (t2v) generation, where DiT [40]
has been proven effective in generating high-quality im-
ages [16] and videos [62, 70] that are coherent with multi-
modality conditions. Thus, we first propose the Holistic
Human DiT (H 2-DiT) upon an existing t2v backbone and
make concise yet principal modifications by involving the
conditions of audio, appearance, and other functional in-
puts, facilitating effective audio-driven animation that con-
sistently preserves the target’s appearance and produces
audio-coherent movements.
However, even large-scale video generation models can-
not handle delicate human details such as fingers. Simi-
larly, our H 2-DiT might produce blurry results on details,
particularly when the predicted gestures are complicated.
We observe that while the generated results may lack pre-
cision in complex regions such as hands and faces, they
can be effectively refined using localized 3D reconstruction
methods [15, 39], which provide rich structural information.
Rather than explicitly utilizing intermediate 3D representa-
tions as direct guidance for whole-body movement gener-
ation [27, 76], which can constrain the natural dynamics
of body motion, we instead leverage these structural priors
solely for localized refinement. The rendered 3D parts offer
explicit guidance by supplying sufficient structural informa-
tion, which benefits more fine-grained regional synthesis on
the corresponding local areas. We thereby modify the DiT
structure to a Regional-Refinement DiT (R2-DiT) that takes
the 3D guidance to produce more realistic results with in-
tricate details. Experiments show that the results from our
cascaded DiTs clearly outperform previous studies.
Our contributions can be summarized as follows: 1) We
propose the AudCast framework to tackle the task of audio-
driven human video generation, which produces expressive
co-speech human videos with intricate details. 2) With
straightforward yet effective design choices, we develop the
Holistic Human DiT, which integrates multi-modal condi-
tions and key foundational components to enable general-
ized, long-duration human video generation with rhythmic
motion and expressive expressions. 3) Leveraging local 3D
reconstructions, we propose the Regional Refinement DiT, a
model that enhances the outputs of the holistic video gener-
ation through a cascaded process informed by 3D structural
priors. This refinement yields noticeable improvements in
fine details of the face and hands.
raw_sections.method:
3. Methodology
This work tackles the challenge of holistic audio-driven hu-
man video generation in a realistic style. Given processed
audio features a = {ats , · · · , ate}, which serve as the driv-
ing signals, and a single reference frame Iref providing ap-
pearance information, our objective is to generate vivid hu-
man videos with natural motion that aligns with the driving
audio while maintaining visual consistency with the refer-
ence frame. Here, ts and te represent the corresponding
time stamps.
In this work, we leverage a pretrained 3D Causal
V AE [62] for video compression, where the encoder and
decoder are represented by E and D, respectively. Given
a target video clip V = {Its , · · · , Ite}, our method oper-
ates on the compressed visual latent features, represented
by Z = E(V). During training, our model is optimized to
restore the video latent ˆZ from the noised latentZ′, which is
produced through a predefined diffusion process [24]. The
restored latent is then decoded to reconstruct the video in
pixel space as ˆV = D(ˆZ).
raw_sections.experiments:
4. Experiments
In this section, we begin by outlining the experimental
setup, including implementation details and the baselines
for comparison. Next, we comprehensively present both
quantitative and qualitative results to showcase the perfor-
mance of our method. Finally, we perform an ablation study
to assess each component in our designs.
Implementation Details. Our model is built based on suc-
cess in general video generation, with 30 stacked MM-DiT-
like Transformer layers. We initialize parts of our model
with pretrained weights from the CogVideoX-2B [62]. Our
model training is performed on a carefully curated dataset of
about 300 hours of videos, specifically collected to feature
10682

specifically to the subject Oliver [1]. All other results are obtained on a combined test set, which includes our dataset alongside video
samples from PATs [1]. Ablation results are listed in the bottom rows, please refer to Sec. 4.3 for the notations.

Ours 0.8275 0.1368 45.41 37.45 527.24 0.2355 0.9485 0.8832 0.3401 0.7454
w/o R2-DiT 0.8118 0.1399 48.27 37.57 549.01 0.2330 0.9428 0.8579 0.3319 0.7248
w/o MT 0.8023 0.1795 45.75 37.52 629.30 0.2343 0.9424 0.8283 0.3264 0.7376

speaking humans positioned centrally within the frame. The
training process spans about 14 days on 8 A100 GPUs us-
ing a learning rate of 5e−5. We evaluate our method under
a zero-shot protocol, meaning that the test inputs, includ-
ing driving audio and reference frames, are entirely unseen
during training, and there is no overlap in the subjects be-
tween training and testing data. The test set consists of a
diverse selection of video clips and frame-audio pairs: 50
clips from 10 subjects in videos collected by us, 20 clips
from 4 subjects sourced from the PATs [1], and 10 frame-
audio pairs collected from the homepage of Vlogger [13]
(Vlogger demo set). Each video or audio clip lasts 3 ∼ 10
seconds.
Comparison Methods. Generalized audio-driven hu-
man video generation is rarely explored in existing re-
search, so we primarily combine methods from two as-
pects as baselines. For audio-driven human gesture genera-
tion, we employ methods including TALKSHOW [64] and
ProbTalk [36], which synthesize human gestures based on
audio inputs. The generated outputs are then converted from
a 3D parametric format to human skeletons, aligning with
the driving representation used in pose-driven human video
reenactment approaches. In the second aspect, we incorpo-
rate two open-source models: ControlNext [41] and Mim-
icMotion [69]. Both are trained on extensive human videos,
enabling zero-shot reenactment. Based on these models, we
have four combined baselines, enabling generalized audio-
driven human video generation for our comparisons. We de-
note them as Prob-CNxt, Prob-Mimic, Talk-CNxt, Talk-
Mimic, repsectively. Comparisons with Vlogger [13] are
also demonstrated in our experiments. We download their
results with reference images and driving audios from their
homepage for fair comparisons.
We also include a comparison with a co-speech genera-
tion method S2G [21], which is optimized for capturing the
motion patterns of a specific subject. Using the official im-
plementation of their model, we conduct evaluation on the
subject Oliver [1]. It is important to note that our method
remains in a zero-shot setting throughout this comparison,
with no tuning specifically for this subject.
raw_sections.conclusion:
5. Conclusion
In this paper, we present a novel framework, AudCast, de-
signed to tackle the challenging task of audio-driven human
video generation. Our approach excels in producing high-
quality human videos that feature synchronized lip move-
ments and natural, rhythmic body motions aligned with au-
dio input. Extensive comparisons with existing methods
demonstrate the effectiveness of our cascaded DiT archi-
tecture, offering valuable insights and setting a foundation
for future research in conditional human video generation
under more intricate scenarios.
